text: The surgeon divides the gastrocolic ligament to enter the lesser sac, exposing the area as much as possible. The procedure progresses towards the duodenum, dividing the fused gastrocolic ligament and exposing the right gastroepiploic vessels.

The surgeon approaches the duodenum, working to mobilize both the duodenum and the uncinate process, likely using surgical instruments to manipulate and separate these structures from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon identifies and divides a branch of the vein leading to the colon, continuing the dissection to expose the right gastroepiploic artery.
The surgeon visually identifies the right gastroepiploic artery, which is exposed during the dissection.
The surgeon assesses the length of the artery, noting its small size, and proceeds to cauterize it using an electrocautery device.
The surgeon divides the remaining ligament attached to the first portion of the duodenum, while several smaller gastroepiploic veins are cauterized.
The surgeon continues to expose the first portion of the duodenum, ensuring it is fully visible within the surgical field.
The surgeon dissects towards the lesser curvature of the duodenum, revealing the gastroduodenal artery (GDA) clearly visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon controls a small bleeder and proceeds to dissect towards the gastro-hepatic ligament, revealing the anatomical structures in the area.
The surgeon continues to dissect the duodenum, progressing until reaching the opposite side, where the liver becomes visible.
The surgeon clears the duodenum below the pylorus and prepares to divide it. The assistant brings in the GIA stapler to perform the division of the duodenum.
The surgeon begins dissection below the pancreas, preparing for a gastropancreatostomy. The pancreas is visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon performs an extensive dissection to the left of the superior mesenteric vein (SMV), carefully separating tissue in this area.
The inferior mesenteric vein (IMV) is visible as the surgeon continues dissection behind the pancreas, progressing towards the clear identification of the porous splenic junction.
The surgeon divides a small branch to the pancreas, possibly a transverse vein, and continues the dissection to expose the superior mesenteric vein (SMV).
The surgeon identifies a cyst abutting the superior mesenteric vein (SMV) in the head of the pancreas. The lack of significant adhesions allows for the vein to be easily freed from the pancreas.
The splenic vein origin is well exposed, with a large cyst visibly pushing against the portal vein and superior mesenteric vein (SMV), though it does not adhere to the vein.

The surgeon continues dissection around the uncinate process, carefully separating the tissue wall from the vein. The dissection progresses distally, revealing the gastrocolic vein branching off from the superior mesenteric vein (SMV).
The surgeon continues dissection down to the uncinate process, with the duodenum clearly exposed, revealing its anatomical position relative to the surrounding structures.
The surgeon approaches the left side of the mesentery root, revealing the fourth portion of the duodenum.
With the duodenum and head of the pancreas well exposed, the surgeon continues the dissection to further mobilize these structures, ensuring clear visibility and access.

The surgeon carefully pushes the superior mesenteric vein (SMV) and its branches away from the uncinate process, continuing the dissection with slow, deliberate movements. A cyst is visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies the first jejunal branch vein and carefully preserves it during the dissection.
The surgeon attempts to mobilize the duodenum, which appears free, but encounters difficulty in repositioning it into the left and right spaces.
The surgeon attempts to mobilize the jejunum on the left side, preparing to divide it, while ensuring maximum tissue mobilization.

The surgeon approaches the left side, where the isocolon has already been divided, allowing for easy identification of the duodenal-jejunal junction. The junction is mobilized from its retroperitoneal attachment.
The surgeon identifies the transfigurements to be divided, noting the minimal presence of fat in the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies the final attachment of the jejunum, preparing to divide it at the designated area.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to devascularize the jejunum, ensuring the blood supply is safely managed during mobilization.
The surgeon uses a PK device to individually cauterize visible vessels, taking time to ensure precise hemostasis during the procedure.
The surgeon divides the branch to the uncinate process on the right side of the mesentery, using cautery to precisely target the vessels while sharply cutting through the remaining tissue.
The surgeon preserves the first jejunal branch due to its size, while selectively removing branches to the pancreas and duodenum. The surgical team works to mobilize the surrounding tissue.

The surgeon carefully separates the mesopancreas from the superior mesenteric artery (SMA), using a suction device to gently push against the adjacent vein while maintaining caution to avoid vascular injury.
The surgeon carefully uses cautery to manage individual vessels, visible as they proceed slowly through the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies a small pancreatodontal artery, which is manageable with cautery, and continues to separate the mesopancreas from the artery.
The surgeon cauterizes individual vessels near the vein, while the neck of the pancreas remains connected. The procedure continues smoothly without any visible complications.
The surgeon continues to work in the space around the neck of the pancreas, maintaining focus on the dissection without dividing the neck at this stage.

The surgeon successfully separates the pancreas from the surrounding artery and vein, with the head of the pancreas now completely detached from these vessels. The surgical field reveals the foramen of Winslow, with the aorta visible in the background.

The surgeon identifies the lymph node of the hepatoduodenal ligament and the superior pancreatoduodenal vein, which is the last vein connecting the pancreas to the portal vein. The vein is cauterized, and the lymph nodes are divided, further detaching the pancreas, leaving only the neck of the pancreas connected.

The surgeon identifies the hepatoduodenal ligament and searches for the gastroduodenal artery. The common bile duct is visible, along with a clip on the remnant of the cystic duct and artery. The surgeon prepares to divide the common duct above the cystic duct junction.
The surgeon continues dissection to gain additional length of the structure, attempting to apply a clip. However, the clip is insufficiently large, necessitating further dissection.

The surgeon divides the cystic duct stump from the cystic artery stump, noting the substantial size of the cystic artery. The common duct is temporarily clipped to prevent leakage before being divided.
The surgeon prepares to divide the pancreatic neck, having previously detached the pancreas from surrounding vessels and structures.

The surgeon continues dissection in the area until the portal vein is visible, indicating that the pancreatic neck is completely isolated. The thin pancreas is divided using a GIA stapler, and one of the branches to the gastroduodenal artery (GDA) is cut.

The surgeon continues dissection to gain more space, deciding between stitches, clips, or cauterization. The gastroduodenal artery (GDA) is clipped, and the pancreatic neck is divided using a GIA stapler to expedite the procedure.

The pancreas is completely detached, leaving the gastroduodenal artery (GDA) and its attachment to the distal common duct. The surgeon prepares to sharply dissect and divide the GDA, freeing the specimen.

The surgeon completes the dissection of the remaining lymph node attachments, successfully removing the specimen. The pancreas is further mobilized to allow rotation and tension-free anastomosis through the stomach.
The surgeon measures approximately four to five centimeters of the pancreatic stump, stopping at the point where the splenic artery curves into the pancreas, preparing to roll back the stump.
The surgeon examines the pancreatic stump, noting the duct's diameter is approximately four to five millimeters.

The surgeon locates the posterior wall of the stomach, positioning it over the pancreas in its natural alignment. The pancreatic duct is identified, measuring approximately four to five millimeters in diameter. The surgeon prepares for an anastomosis using a traditional two-layer technique, first attaching the capsule to the seromuscular layer.
The surgeon sutures the pancreatic duct mucosa to the gastric mucosa using silk sutures, as V-lock sutures are not available.
The surgeon opens the stomach to perform a gastrotomy, ensuring the mucosal opening is just large enough to accommodate the pancreatic duct for anastomosis.

The surgeon aligns the pancreatic duct with the gastric mucosa and begins suturing the posterior wall in an interrupted fashion. The duct is of a good size, allowing for the stitch to pass through without the need for a stent.
The surgeon performs ductomucosa anastomosis, placing stitches through the pancreatic duct to connect it to the gastric mucosa. The process involves several stitches to secure the anastomosis.

The surgeon uses 3-0 Vicryl sutures to add a few more stitches, achieving a precise approximation of the pancreatic mucosa to the gastric mucosa. The focus then shifts to the anterior wall anastomosis.
The surgeon places three interrupted stitches to secure the duct mucosa of the pancreas to the gastric mucosa, ensuring visibility of the duct during the anastomosis.
The surgeon ties the sutures, proceeding to the second layer of the anterior anastomosis, connecting the cervical muscular layer to the capsule of the pancreas.

The surgeon completes the anterior anastomosis, ensuring the pancreatic and gastric mucosa come together smoothly without tension. The pancreas is mobilized adequately to facilitate this alignment. The procedure progresses to the common duct anastomosis.

The surgeon assesses the size of the duct, noting it is large, and begins the posterior anastomosis using 5-0 PDS sutures in a running fashion. A stay suture is used to stabilize the jejunum during the procedure.

The surgeon performs a jitrinostomy anastomosis, also known as Lassa anastomosis, in an end-to-side configuration with two layers. A 3-0 PDS suture is used for the posterior layer, attaching the staple line of the duodenum to the seromuscular layer of the jejunum. After completing the posterior seromuscular anastomosis, the duodenum and jejunum are opened for a full-thickness posterior anastomosis using the same suture. The specimen is then removed through an enlarged umbilical incision.
